most corpus instances have मुटु, but there is one where definitely the organ and not an emotion is referred to and where the translation is कलेजो. In Yakkha the meaning of the word is कलेजो rather than मुटु , and मुटु is an erroneous translation based on the fact that the heart rather than the liver is viewed as the origin of human emotions in Nepali. The transcribers also suggested कलेजो, but this is also their translation for coʔwa, which they translate as 'heart' in another line in the same question file, so they are probably simply clueless. मुटु has been chosen as the final translation because it was in the community dictionary and because luŋmak is the only word that CAN be translated as 'heart' (meaning that if luŋmak wasn't 'heart' there wouldn't be a word for 'heart').

Werner (2003): A Bantawa Dictionary taŋ 'head'; there seems to be no word raŋ in Bantawa! This is likely to be a variant that is used in compounds. Bantawa has t > d > r / V_V (e.g. *khat-a-ŋ > Chintang khadaŋ, Bantawa kharaŋ), and -raŋ is also the Sambugaũ variant of -taŋ in tree names (also cf. Mulgaũ sɨŋtaŋ : Sambugaũ sɨŋraŋ). chadiwaraŋ seems to be a compound (parallel to nambataŋ), so the rule would be applicable.

meaning still not fully clear. This is mostly due to the fact that it's not sure whether सोल्टी is a good translation for this, and if so, what's its precise meaning - Turner, CLS and BND give diverging translations. The present gloss tries to make sense of all these translations but is admittedly constructed.

Sakẽwa or, in other Kirãti languages, Sakela is also the name for the Udhouli and Ubhouli rituals traditionally performed when moving to the lowlands or highlands between seasons. In Chintang, rituals are performed at the Sakẽwa altar at the beginning of Wadhaŋmi. If there's a connection between Wadhaŋmi and Sakẽwa in other Kirãti groups, the word "sakẽwa" could first have been the name of the ritual and later been transferred to the place by metonymy.

Question: Out of the only two corpus instances where som appears outside som si-/som set- one instance has 'liver'/कलेजो in the translation, the other 'lung'/फोकसो. An informant also said that som and somphorok ('lung'/फोक्सो) are more or less synonyms (+ cf. their etymological relation). On the other hand, the translation as 'liver'/कलेजो fits better with the use in som si-/som set-, as the liver is more commonly viewed as the seat of emotions. कलेजो is also the translation in the community dictionary. It has been chosen as the final version here because there are no good competitors for कलेजो.
